skill.name: team-collaboration-issue
skill.description: Guides AI assistants through GitHub issue triage, branch workflow, implementation, testing, pull request creation, and verification.
skill.summary: Resolve GitHub issues with structured investigation, implementation, testing, and pull request workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected many command, network, weak cryptography, sensitive-data, and reconnaissance patterns. Review found the confirmed risk is operational command execution in Markdown workflow examples for GitHub issue resolution, while the weak cryptography, sensitive-data, reconnaissance, and obfuscation signals are false positives from placeholders or normal development examples. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious obfuscation evidence was found.

medium_findings.title: External Command Workflow Requires User Oversight
medium_findings.description: The skill provides examples that run GitHub CLI, git, npm, grep, and shell scripts to inspect issues, create branches, push changes, run checks, create pull requests, and close issues. These commands are appropriate for the stated purpose, but they can modify repositories and remote GitHub state if executed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in Markdown examples and align with the skill purpose. Risk comes from real side effects such as branch creation, pushes, pull requests, and issue updates, not from hidden malicious code.

medium_findings.title: Network-Connected GitHub and Health Check Examples
medium_findings.description: The playbook includes GitHub CLI operations and a curl health check. These are legitimate for issue resolution, but they contact external services and should require normal permission checks before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is explicit and expected for GitHub collaboration. I did not find evidence that it sends secrets or data to an unauthorized destination.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections correspond to issue numbers, placeholder strings, test descriptions, or PR template text, not cryptographic algorithms or key generation code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithm use in executable skill logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible prose, placeholders, issue identifiers, or test headings. They do not implement or recommend weak cryptography.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Data and Reconnaissance Static Hits Are Contextual Checks
low_findings.description: The sensitive-data and reconnaissance detections are caused by dependency inspection, grep commands that check for secrets before a pull request, issue investigation commands, and SQL/query examples. These are defensive review patterns rather than credential access or system probing for malicious use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is framed as normal debugging, validation, testing, or secret-prevention guidance. I did not find instructions to collect credentials or perform unauthorized reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Side-Effecting Shell Commands in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The playbook includes command examples for creating branches, pushing to origin, creating pull requests, commenting on issues, closing issues, and running scripts. Agents should request confirmation before executing commands that change local or remote state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are concrete and can have side effects when copied into a terminal. They are still consistent with the GitHub issue workflow described by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Pull Request Body Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The PR creation example uses shell command substitution to build a multi-line pull request body. This is a common shell pattern, but any interpolated issue data or generated text should be reviewed before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and can be risky if untrusted content is inserted into shell context. The example itself uses static template text and does not show malicious injection.

content.value_statement: GitHub issues often lack enough structure for reliable implementation. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through triage, fixes, tests, pull requests, and verification.
actual_capabilities: Guides issue triage with severity classification and context gathering.
Suggests branch naming, commit strategy, and GitHub issue linking patterns.
Provides test planning examples for unit, integration, and end-to-end coverage.
Outlines pull request preparation, description structure, and review checklist steps.
Defines post-implementation verification and issue closure workflows.
limitations: Requires access to the target repository and GitHub issue details.
Does not replace project-specific coding standards or reviewer approval.
Command examples may change local or remote repository state if executed.
Does not include executable automation scripts or direct GitHub API integration.

use_cases.title: Triage a vague bug report
use_cases.description: Classify severity, gather related issues, inspect recent changes, and form a clear investigation plan.

use_cases.title: Implement a tracked feature request
use_cases.description: Break work into phases, create a branch, add tests, implement changes, and prepare a reviewable pull request.

use_cases.title: Prepare a reliable hotfix workflow
use_cases.description: Investigate a regression, isolate the cause, add verification, and document rollback steps for reviewers.

prompt_templates.title: Start Issue Triage
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner triage for one GitHub issue.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to triage issue #123. Summarize the reported problem, likely affected areas, missing information, and next investigation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an Implementation
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a practical plan before editing code.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill for issue #123. Build an implementation plan with branch name, test strategy, files to inspect, and review checkpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Fix and Verify a Bug
prompt_templates.scenario: Guide development after the root cause is known.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to resolve issue #123. Implement the fix, add focused tests, run relevant checks, and prepare a concise verification summary.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Pull Request Handoff
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to prepare the final handoff for issue #123. Include root cause, changes, tests, risk, rollback notes, and a pull request description.

output_examples.input: Triage issue #456 about failed login after the latest release.
output_examples.output: The assistant classifies the issue as high priority, lists likely authentication areas, identifies recent commits to inspect, and proposes a failing test before implementation.

output_examples.input: Prepare a pull request summary for a completed profile settings fix.
Create an implementation plan for a slow dashboard query issue.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends profiling the query, adding a regression test, optimizing the data access path, measuring before and after results, and documenting performance impact.

best_practices: Confirm issue scope, repository state, and user approval before running side-effecting commands.
Create focused tests that reproduce the issue before changing production code.
Link commits, pull requests, verification notes, and issue closure comments clearly.
anti_patterns: Do not close issues without documenting the root cause and verification results.
Do not run push, pull request, or issue mutation commands without review.
Do not rely on generated code changes without project-specific tests and CI checks.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps AI assistants investigate GitHub issues, plan fixes, add tests, prepare pull requests, and document verification.

faq.question: Does it automatically fix issues?
faq.answer: No. It provides a structured workflow and examples. The assistant still needs repository access and user approval for changes.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is written as guidance that can support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: Does it require GitHub CLI?
faq.answer: Many examples use GitHub CLI, but the process can be adapted to other GitHub tools or manual review steps.

faq.question: What security precautions are needed?
faq.answer: Review commands before execution, especially commands that push branches, create pull requests, comment on issues, or call external URLs.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production hotfixes?
faq.answer: It includes hotfix workflow examples, but production fixes still require team approval, tests, CI checks, and rollback planning.
